target.title: fix(tools): let apply_patch inherit trusted exec mode
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `apply_patch` stayed workspace-contained even when `exec` was explicitly configured as `security=full` and `ask=off`, forcing trusted owner/operator setups to manage a second, protected `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` knob.
- Why it matters: no-approval full exec can already write anywhere the selected host permits, so making `apply_patch` stricter in that mode is inconsistent friction rather than a meaningful security boundary.
- What changed: when `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset, `apply_patch` now inherits configured no-approval full-exec host-write authority; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still force workspace containment.
- What did NOT change: `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` remains an explicit dangerous config flag, and approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #77886
- Related #62006
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `apply_patch` outside the workspace should work when the operator has already configured trusted no-approval full exec (`tools.exec.security=full`, `tools.exec.ask=off`) and has not explicitly forced `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux, Node 24, branch `seb/apply-patch-inherits-full-exec`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```sh
  PATH="/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/Cellar/node@24/24.15.0/bin:$PATH" pnpm exec tsx - <<'TS'
  import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
  import os from 'node:os';
  import path from 'node:path';
  import { createOpenClawCodingTools } from './src/agents/pi-tools.ts';

  const workspace = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-real-applypatch-'));
  const escaped = path.join(path.dirname(workspace), `openclaw-real-applypatch-${process.pid}.txt`);
  const rel = path.relative(workspace, escaped);
  try {
    const tools = createOpenClawCodingTools({
      workspaceDir: workspace,
      agentDir: workspace,
      config: { tools: { allow: ['read', 'write', 'exec'], exec: { security: 'full', ask: 'off' } } },
      modelProvider: 'openai',
      modelId: 'gpt-5.5',
    });
    const tool = tools.find((t) => t.name === 'apply_patch');
    if (!tool) throw new Error('apply_patch missing');
    await (tool as any).execute('manual-check', {
      input: `*** Begin Patch\n*** Add File: ${rel}\n+ok\n*** End Patch`,
    });
    const content = await fs.readFile(escaped, 'utf8');
    console.log(JSON.stringify({ workspace, escapedExists: content === 'ok\n', content }));
  } finally {
    await fs.rm(escaped, { force: true });
    await fs.rm(workspace, { recursive: true, force: true });
  }
  TS
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  ```json
  {"workspace":"/tmp/openclaw-real-applypatch-WfVLht","escapedExists":true,"content":"ok\n"}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: the real `apply_patch` tool instance wrote the file outside the temporary workspace under configured `security=full` + `ask=off`.
- What was not tested: a full live Gateway restart/session using this source checkout; the live installed Gateway was not modified.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): existing regression coverage documents the old behavior in `pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`: absent an explicit `workspaceOnly=false`, outside-workspace `apply_patch` rejected with `Path escapes sandbox root`.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `createOpenClawCodingTools()` computed `applyPatchWorkspaceOnly` solely from `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` and `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly`, ignoring the effective configured exec trust mode.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`, but not the coherent trusted-exec case (`security=full`, `ask=off`).
- Contributing context (if known): `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` is correctly treated as dangerous/protected for agent-initiated config mutation, which made the duplicate knob especially awkward for owner-operated trusted deployments.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: full exec with approvals off allows outside-workspace `apply_patch` when `applyPatch.workspaceOnly` is unset; full exec with approvals on still blocks; explicit `applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still block.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the actual tool construction path and `apply_patch` execution without requiring a Gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for trusted exec inheritance; existing explicit opt-out test remains.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- In configured `tools.exec.security=full` + `tools.exec.ask=off` mode, `apply_patch` can write outside the workspace without also setting `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false`.
- Operators can still force workspace containment with `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or the umbrella `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
exec full/off -> shell can write outside workspace
exec full/off -> apply_patch still blocked unless applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false

After:
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly unset -> apply_patch inherits host-write authority
exec full/off + applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true or tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true -> still workspace-contained
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: This intentionally makes `apply_patch` match an already trusted/no-approval full-exec configuration. In that mode, `exec` can already write arbitrary files using shell commands. Mitigations: approval/allowlist exec modes still keep `apply_patch` workspace-contained by default; explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` and `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` override inheritance and force workspace containment.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: direct local source checkout, Node 24
- Model/provider: OpenAI provider/tool registration path (`modelProvider: openai`, `modelId: gpt-5.5`)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `{ "tools": { "allow": ["read", "write", "exec"], "exec": { "security": "full", "ask": "off" } } }`

### Steps

1. Create a temporary workspace.
2. Construct OpenClaw coding tools with `tools.exec.security=full` and `tools.exec.ask=off`.
3. Invoke `apply_patch` to add a file outside the workspace via a relative `..` path.

### Expected

- `apply_patch` writes the file because no-approval full exec already grants equivalent host-write authority.

### Actual

- The file was created outside the workspace and read back as `ok\n`.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted checks run:

```sh
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-gateway-tool.test.ts src/security/dangerous-config-flags.test.ts
# Test Files 7 passed (7); Tests 140 passed (140)

pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/pi-tools-agent-config.test.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/schema.help.ts docs/tools/apply-patch.md docs/tools/exec.md
# All matched files use the correct format.

pnpm check:changed
# exited 0
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - trusted `security=full` + `ask=off` inherits host-write authority for `apply_patch` when unset.
  - `security=full` + `ask=on-miss` remains workspace-contained by default.
  - `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
  - explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` still forces workspace containment.
- Edge cases checked: existing explicit `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=false` behavior still works; gateway protected-config tests still pass unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live installed Gateway behavior after replacing the installed OpenClaw package with this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for approval/allowlist/default workspace-contained modes; intentional behavior change for configured no-approval full exec.
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: deployments with `security=full` + `ask=off` that expected `apply_patch` to remain workspace-only when unset will see broader `apply_patch` writes.
  - Mitigation: set `tools.exec.applyPatch.workspaceOnly=true` or `tools.fs.workspaceOnly=true`; docs and schema help now call this out.

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): onModelDiagnosticEvent for trusted model.* events
target.body:
## Why

Plugins subscribing to diagnostic events via `onDiagnosticEvent` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime`) never see the `model.*` lifecycle. The public API filter at [`src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879-886`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts#L879-L886):

```ts
export function onDiagnosticEvent(listener: (evt: DiagnosticEventPayload) => void): () => void {
  return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
    if (metadata.trusted || event.type === "log.record") {
      return;
    }
    listener(event);
  });
}
```

drops **every** event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. The runtime emits `model.call.started`, `model.call.completed`, `model.call.error`, `model.usage`, and `model.failover` via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent`. So the entire model lifecycle is invisible to plugins.

This breaks observability/cost-attribution plugins. A telemetry bridge plugin needs:

- `model.call.completed` (provider/model identity) to populate per-call span attributes
- `model.usage` (token counts, cost) for per-call attribution

Today both subscriptions are dead code against the trusted emit paths. Concretely, the embedded codex/Responses runner emits the full model lifecycle via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent` (see `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts`), but no plugin listener ever receives any of it — model traffic on the dashboard side ends up bucketed as "unknown" instead of `gpt-5.5`/etc.

## Approach

Add a focused, opt-in plugin-sdk API `onModelDiagnosticEvent` that exposes exactly the five model lifecycle event types — and only those — to plugins. The general `onDiagnosticEvent` filter is unchanged: it still drops trusted events, so the safety contract of the broad subscription is preserved. Plugins that want model telemetry have to opt in explicitly.

The new helper otherwise mirrors `onDiagnosticEvent`'s contract (cloned-and-frozen payloads, isolated listener failures, unsubscribe disposer).

A module-local allowlist (`PLUGIN_VISIBLE_TRUSTED_MODEL_EVENT_TYPES`) documents which event types are reachable through this API and is the single place to update if more model lifecycle types are added later.

## Files

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts` — add `onModelDiagnosticEvent` + allowlist (+45 lines)
- `src/plugin-sdk/{diagnostic-runtime,index,compat}.ts` — re-export the new function so plugins can `import { onModelDiagnosticEvent } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime"` (or the top-level barrel / compat subpath)
- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — three new tests:
  1. delivers all five `model.*` trusted types and filters non-model traffic
  2. the original `onDiagnosticEvent` still hides model events from plugins (defends the safety contract)
  3. unsubscribe disposer stops further delivery
- `CHANGELOG.md` — Unreleased / Changes entry

Total: 6 files, +158 / −2.

## Tests

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts`: 22/22 passing
- Plugin-SDK guardrail tests (`src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts`): 44 tests, all passing

## End-to-end validation

Built a full `dist/` from this branch, swapped it into a deployed gateway, and ran a real codex (`openai-codex/gpt-5.5`) turn from a Telegram-driven session. Bridge plugin logs show **both** subscriptions on startup:

```
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
```

…and during the turn the bridge now receives the `model.usage` event for codex/gpt-5.5 (previously dead-code path):

```
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
```

The downstream dashboard bucket that had been showing "unknown" for every codex turn now correctly attributes to `gpt-5.5`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Plugins subscribing via the public `onDiagnosticEvent` API never observe trusted `model.*` lifecycle events (`model.call.started/completed/error`, `model.usage`, `model.failover`) because the existing filter at `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879` drops every event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. On my deployed install, this surfaced as Codex `gpt-5.5` turns bucketing as "unknown" in a downstream observability bridge plugin (agentweave-bridge) — the per-turn span never received `prov.llm.{provider,model}` because the bridge's `model.usage` / `model.call.completed` handlers were dead code against the trusted emit paths.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw v2026.5.10-beta.1 gateway running under `systemctl --user` on Linux (Debian 12, Node 24.13 via nvm) on my NAS (192.168.1.70), driving a Codex subscription session via Telegram channel and the embedded pi-runner. Local install built from this PR head: `dist/` produced by `pnpm build` from commit 52f87ce58a, swapped into `/home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/`, gateway restarted. Bridge plugin installed under `~/.openclaw/user-plugins/agentweave-bridge/`, rebuilt against the new SDK export.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  # 1. Build the patched openclaw from this branch
  cd /home/Arnab/dev/openclaw-model-usage
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  CI=true pnpm build

  # 2. Install over the canonical openclaw dist/
  systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway
  mv /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist.bak
  cp -r dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist
  systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway

  # 3. Confirm trust-gated body in the built artifact
  grep -A2 "function onModelDiagnosticEvent" \
    /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/diagnostic-events-*.js

  # 4. Drive a real Codex turn through Telegram and observe the bridge plugin logs
  journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway -f | grep agentweave-bridge
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  - Built-artifact contains the trust-gated callback:
    ```
    function onModelDiagnosticEvent(listener) {
      return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
        if (!metadata.trusted) return;
    ```
  - Gateway startup logs from `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` show the plugin subscribing to BOTH streams (previously only one):
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] OTel SDK started, exporting to: http://192.168.1.70:30418/v1/traces
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
    ```
  - During a real Telegram-initiated Codex turn (openai-codex/gpt-5.5), the bridge plugin now receives a trusted `model.usage` event that was previously invisible to it:
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:main agent: nix-v1
    [agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
    [agentweave-bridge] ended root span for session: agent:main:main
    ```
- Observed result after fix: The downstream observability dashboard that had been bucketing every Codex turn as "unknown" model now correctly attributes them to `gpt-5.5`, with cost figures flowing through the bridge's pricing fallback. No behavior change for any other plugin: the broad `onDiagnosticEvent` subscription still filters every trusted event as before (defended by the regression test `onDiagnosticEvent still hides model.* trusted events from plugins`). A separate regression test confirms forged untrusted `model.*` events from `emitDiagnosticEvent` do NOT reach the new opt-in subscription.
- What was not tested: I did not exercise this against an isolated subagent (`agent:isolated:*`) session, only `agent:main:main`. Did not run on macOS or Windows; tested under Linux Node 24.13 only. Did not run the live `pnpm test` matrix exhaustively — the four review-required test files all pass locally (`src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` 23/23, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-index.test.ts` 5/5, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts` 13/13, `src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts` 8/8) plus `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` and `pnpm build` are clean.

target.title: fix(matrix): add async shared-client stop helpers awaiting final persist
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The synchronous `stopSharedClient`, `stopSharedClientForAccount`, and `stopSharedClientInstance` helpers in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts` kick off the final crypto/sync persist via `MatrixClient.stop()` but return before it completes, so a process exit right after a stop can lose recently negotiated Olm/Megolm session keys.
- Why it matters: Lost crypto state shows up after restart as `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops and repeated "Established new Olm sessions" against the same sender.
- What changed: Added async `stopSharedClientAsync`, `stopSharedClientForAccountAsync`, and `stopSharedClientInstanceAsync` that await `client.stopAndPersist()`. Marked the legacy synchronous helpers `@deprecated`. Re-exported the two account-scoped async helpers from the matrix barrel. Added three Vitest cases. Added one CHANGELOG line.
- What did NOT change: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts` was not touched. `MatrixClient.stop()` already triggers the final persist and `stopAndPersist()` already exists; the periodic 60s timer and the four crypto-event bindings at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/decrypt-bridge.ts:153-164` were already wired.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #76611 
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: this PR narrowly fixes the synchronous stop-helper persistence defect from #76611 only. The event-driven crypto-state persist defect (`KeyBackupDecryptionKeyCached`, `RehydrationCompleted`, `DevicesUpdated`, `KeysChanged` not driving `persistIdbToDisk`) remains open and is not addressed here.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Sync helpers return `void` and call `client.stop()`, which assigns `stopPersistPromise` for the final IDB/sync flush but does not return it.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test pinned that shared-client teardown produces a settled persist promise; no `@deprecated` signal steered callers toward the existing async path.
- Contributing context: `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` and `MatrixClient.stopAndPersist()` already exist as the safe path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario: Each new async helper invokes `client.stopAndPersist()` exactly once on the targeted client(s) and does not call `client.stop()`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The shared-client registry is the single chokepoint between callers and `MatrixClient`.
- Existing test that already covers this: None for the async-stop helpers.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None for end users. Plugin authors who deep-import `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client.ts` get three new async exports; legacy synchronous exports keep their current signatures.

## Diagram

N/A.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Net effect on security posture is positive: hardens the Matrix E2EE crypto-store shutdown path so recently negotiated session keys are durable across restarts when the new helpers are adopted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11; fix is platform-agnostic.
- Runtime: Node 22.
- Integration: Matrix (`extensions/matrix`).

### Steps

1. Acquire a shared Matrix client through `resolveSharedMatrixClient` for an E2EE-enabled account.
2. Trigger a shutdown path that calls a synchronous `stopSharedClient*` helper and exits the process immediately.
3. Restart and observe whether incoming E2EE messages decrypt cleanly.

### Expected

- Recently negotiated Olm/Megolm sessions remain decryptable across restart.

### Actual (without this fix)

- Process exits before `stopPersistPromise` resolves; restart loses recent session keys, producing `InvalidMAC(MacError)` loops.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after

Three Vitest cases added in `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts`. CI must run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed`.

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Static review against the existing `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")` precedent at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.ts:295`. Verified the new helpers reuse `deleteSharedClientState` and `removeSharedClientInstance` with the same ordering as the existing async-release path. Verified the mock client in `shared.test.ts` exposes `stopAndPersist` without breaking the existing release-lease test.
- Edge cases checked: Concurrent acquire after a stop drops cleanly because state is removed before the awaited persist. `Promise.all` was chosen for `stopSharedClientAsync` to match the existing error semantics; inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)` at `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk.ts:677-689`.
- What was not verified: Vitest was not executed in the authoring shell (no `pnpm`/`node` on PATH). The reporter's full prod symptom was not reproduced against a live homeserver.

## Review Conversations

- [x] Bot review conversations addressed in this PR will be replied to or resolved.
- [x] Only conversations that still need maintainer judgment will be left open.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A future contributor copies a `@deprecated` synchronous helper into a new shutdown path despite the jsdoc warning.
  - Mitigation: jsdoc on each deprecated helper names the async replacement directly; CHANGELOG entry highlights the shutdown-correctness rationale.
- Risk: `Promise.all` in `stopSharedClientAsync` rejects on the first failing per-client persist.
  - Mitigation: Inner persist work already catches errors with `.catch(noop)`, so `stopAndPersist()` resolves under normal conditions; behavior matches `releaseSharedClientInstance(client, "persist")`.

---

AI-assisted PR (Claude Opus 4.7, 1M context). Lightly tested: static review and trace-through; Vitest not executed locally. Reviewers should run `pnpm test extensions/matrix/src/matrix/client/shared.test.ts` and `pnpm test:changed` before landing.

target.title: fix(zalouser): surface send errors and recover from oversized style payloads
target.body:
## TL;DR

Zalo delivery failures were completely invisible: the monitor never checked send results, error codes were lost, and messages silently disappeared when styled payloads exceeded Zalo's size limit.

---

## Problem

Three compounding gaps caused messages to vanish without any log trace:

1. `sendZaloTextMessage` caught errors but discarded the numeric `error_code` from Zalo's response — callers could not distinguish a transient network error from a Zalo-specific rejection.
2. The `sendText` and `sendMedia` callbacks in the monitor called `sendMessageZalouser` without checking the returned result, so `ok=false` responses were silently dropped.
3. Zalo rejects messages with `error_code=112` when the combined params payload (message text + `textProperties` JSON for styling) exceeds its server-side size limit. Empirically: a ~1420-char Vietnamese message combined with 56+ style ranges (~1871 chars of JSON) triggers this. The message was never delivered and no error appeared in logs.

---

## Invariant

> When Zalo rejects a styled message with error code 112, the message text must still be delivered (without styles), and the failure must be logged if delivery ultimately fails.

---

## What this PR does

- Adds `errorCode?: number` to `ZaloSendResult` so callers can branch on specific Zalo error codes
- Enhances `toErrorMessage` to include the numeric code in the string so it appears in logs
- Extracts the numeric error code from caught errors in `sendZaloTextMessage` and attaches it to the result
- Adds a retry-without-styles path in `sendMessageZalouser` on error code 112: if the styled send fails with 112, the message is re-sent as plain text
- Makes `sendText` and `sendMedia` in the monitor check `result.ok` and emit a structured error log when delivery fails

---

## Why this matters

- **User impact**: Long bot responses with markdown formatting silently failed to reach Zalo users. The bot appeared to respond on the web UI but nothing arrived in the chat.
- **System impact**: Failures were completely unobservable — no logs, no error propagation — making debugging impossible without adding instrumentation manually.

---

## Safety

- No change to message delivery path when everything succeeds
- The retry only triggers on the specific error code 112 with styled content; all other errors are forwarded unchanged
- Existing tests unchanged; 3 new regression tests added

---

## Test plan

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/zalouser/src/send.test.ts
```

- [x] All existing tests pass (13)
- [x] New test: retries without styles on error code 112
- [x] New test: propagates error when retry also fails
- [x] New test: does not retry when there are no styles

target.title: Fix/cron duplicate name validation
target.body:
Fixes #76160

\`openclaw cron add\` and \`cron edit\` now enforce unique job names, preventing silent duplicate-name acceptance that could lead to dual-fire risk when both jobs remain enabled.

## Problem

Previously, the cron service silently accepted jobs with duplicate names:
- Two \`cron add\` calls with the same name created two distinct rows with different IDs
- \`cron edit\` could rename a job onto an existing name
- Default \`cron list\` only showed one job when their \`enabled\` states differed, hiding the divergence
- If both jobs remained enabled, they would fire twice per cycle with potentially divergent prompts

## Solution

Added name uniqueness validation in the service layer:
- **\`cron.add\`**: Checks for existing job with the same name before creating
- **\`cron.update\`**: Checks for name collision when renaming (allows no-op self-rename)
- **Disabled jobs**: Included in uniqueness check to prevent the exact scenario from the bug report
- **Error messages**: Clear, actionable format with existing job ID and removal instructions

## Implementation

- \`src/cron/service/ops.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` checks in \`add()\` and \`update()\`
- \`src/cron/service/jobs.ts\`: Added \`findJobByName()\` helper function
- \`src/cron/service.duplicate-names.test.ts\`: Comprehensive test suite (13 test cases)

All entry points (CLI, agent tool, gateway RPC) funnel through the service layer, so validation is enforced everywhere.

## Testing

Added dedicated test suite covering:
- Adding jobs with duplicate names (enabled and disabled)
- Renaming jobs to existing names (enabled and disabled)
- Edge cases (case sensitivity, special characters, empty names)
- No-op renames (renaming to same name)
- Error message validation

## Breaking Changes

None. This is a bug fix that prevents invalid state. Operators who were accidentally creating duplicate names will now receive clear error messages with instructions to resolve the conflict.

## Related

- Supersedes closed PR #76180
- Found during production config audit in markthebest12/openclaw-infra#1305" --base main

target.title: fix(exec): hide policy controls from model args
target.body:
## Summary

- Remove operator-only exec controls (`security`, `ask`, `host`, `elevated`, `node`) from the model-facing exec tool schema
- Stop consuming those fields from exec tool-call args; configured tool defaults and approval defaults now remain the only policy source
- Move trusted chat command policy overrides into exec defaults instead of per-call args
- Update regression coverage for model-supplied policy fields, configured host/node routing, elevated defaults, and agent-specific exec defaults

Fixes #75811.

## Security

This closes the model-controlled exec policy boundary described in #75811. Model tool calls can still provide command/runtime inputs (`command`, `workdir`, `env`, `yieldMs`, `background`, `timeout`, `pty`), but cannot lower/raise security, approval, host routing, elevation, or node selection through tool arguments.

## Test plan

target.title: [codex] Add plugin task lifecycle API
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Plugins can inspect task runs today, but they do not have a narrow public SDK path for creating a plugin-owned task run, reporting progress, and finalizing the run while preserving owner/session scoping. That leaves plugin task lifecycle behavior either unavailable or tied to internal core task APIs that are not safe for third-party plugin contracts.

This PR adds a plugin-safe task run lifecycle API under `api.runtime.tasks.runs.bindSession(...).lifecycle` / `fromToolContext(...).lifecycle`. Plugin-created records are stored as existing `cli` task runtime rows with a required plugin-namespaced `taskKind`, and task creation identity now includes `taskKind` so same-owner/same-run records with different plugin task kinds stay separated.

## Summary

- Adds a plugin-safe task run lifecycle API under `api.runtime.tasks.runs.bindSession(...).lifecycle` / `fromToolContext(...).lifecycle`.
- Creates plugin-owned records as existing `cli` task runtime rows with required plugin-namespaced `taskKind`.
- Adds owner-scoped exact create/progress/finalize behavior and exposes `taskKind` in task run DTOs.
- Documents plugin-owned task records and SDK lifecycle usage.

## Evidence

Focused validation:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  71 passed (71)
```

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/runtime/runtime-tasks.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  29 passed (29)
```

```text
$ pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256
```

```text
$ pnpm check:changed
[check:changed] lanes=core, coreTests, extensions, extensionTests, docs
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
runtime-sidecar-loaders: local runtime sidecar loaders look OK.
Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```

Additional checks run:

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched files>`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `git diff --check`

Redacted real runtime lifecycle proof at PR head `95cedbf4558905e68f351613f71fe7640df5b69e`, using `createRuntimeTaskRuns().bindSession(...).lifecycle` with an isolated temp state dir:

```text
$ pnpm tsx --eval '<runtime lifecycle create/progress/finalize proof script>'
{
  "created": {
    "runtime": "cli",
    "sessionKey": "agent:proof:telegram:group:redacted",
    "ownerKey": "agent:proof:telegram:group:redacted",
    "taskKind": "proof-plugin.session",
    "sourceId": "proof-plugin",
    "runId": "proof-run-redacted",
    "status": "running",
    "progressSummary": "created"
  },
  "progressed": {
    "runtime": "cli",
    "taskKind": "proof-plugin.session",
    "runId": "proof-run-redacted",
    "status": "running",
    "progressSummary": "progressed"
  },
  "finalized": {
    "runtime": "cli",
    "taskKind": "proof-plugin.session",
    "runId": "proof-run-redacted",
    "status": "succeeded",
    "progressSummary": "progressed",
    "terminalSummary": "finalized"
  },
  "sameKindReuse": {
    "sameTaskId": true,
    "taskKind": "proof-plugin.session",
    "runId": "proof-run-redacted"
  },
  "differentTaskKindSeparation": {
    "differentTaskId": true,
    "firstTaskKind": "proof-plugin.session",
    "secondTaskKind": "proof-plugin.other",
    "sharedRunId": "proof-run-redacted"
  }
}
```

This proves create, progress, finalize, same-kind reuse, and different-`taskKind` separation through the public plugin runtime lifecycle API.

## Known Base Failure

- `pnpm test:changed` routes `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` and fails `loadOpenClawPlugins > does not re-register non-bundled plugins after gateway-bindable boot loads` with expected `1`, received `2`.
- The same failure reproduces on a fresh `origin/main` worktree at `9e01d19db3`, so it is not introduced by this PR.

target.title: fix(outbound): strip inbound metadata before delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

## Changes

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: payload with echoed metadata block → metadata stripped, actual response preserved
- [x] All 9 tests in `payloads.test.ts` pass
- [x] `pnpm build && pnpm check` — clean

> AI-assisted: test case co-authored with Claude (Anthropic).

target.title: fix(qqbot): persist group inbound delivery route for announce target resolution
target.body:
## Summary

The `event.type !== "c2c"` guard at `extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts:444` allows `dm` (direct message) events to also persist route via `updateLastRoute`, because `event.type` can be `"dm"` and `"dm" !== "c2c"` evaluates to `true`. This means QQ Bot direct messages unintentionally overwrite the session deliveryContext, which can cause group announce routing to target the DM conversation instead of the correct group.

Change the guard from `event.type !== "c2c"` to `event.type === "group" || event.type === "guild"`, explicitly gating route persistence to only group and guild turns. DM events (both `c2c` and `dm` types) are unconditionally excluded.

Fixes #94785

---

## What Problem This Solves (可选)

**Problem**: QQ Bot group announce delivery fails in multi-group scenarios because `updateLastRoute` incorrectly persists after non-group events (including `dm` type DMs), overwriting the session deliveryContext and causing downstream announce target resolution to route to the wrong conversation.

**Why This Change Was Made**: The original `!== "c2c"` negative-match is semantically wrong — it only excludes `c2c` but allows `dm` and any future non-group event type through. A positive-match (`=== "group" || === "guild"`) is the correct approach: only explicitly named group conversation types should trigger route persistence. This matches the pattern used in Discord (`isDirectMessage`), iMessage (`!decision.isGroup`), LINE (`!params.source.isGroup`), and Mattermost (`kind === "direct"`).

**User Impact**: Group announce messages (cron job completion, subagent emit, sessions_send) will now consistently route to the correct group conversation instead of falling back to key-parsed heuristics that may resolve to the wrong group. No impact on DM conversations.

---

## Linked context (可选)

- **Issue**: #94785
- **In scope**: Route persistence guard condition in `dispatchOutbound`; 4 new test cases for c2c/dm/group/guild event types
- **Out of scope**: Changes to announce message creation, Gateway session management, npm dependencies, or other QQ Bot dispatch paths

---

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gate QQ Bot route persistence to group and guild event types only, excluding c2c and dm direct messages.

**Real environment tested**: Linux 6.8.0-124-generic / OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (151575f) / Node v25.9.0 / DeepSeek V4 Flash model

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# Start Gateway from worktree with the fix
cd extensions/qqbot
DEEPSEEK_API_KEY="sk-***" pnpm dev gateway

# Send c2c (private) message via QQ Bot
# Send group message via QQ Bot

# Monitor route-persistence logs
cat /tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-06-30.log | grep route-persistence
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
# c2c private message — updateLastRoute correctly skipped
23:35:54 [qqbot] Processing message (type=c2c): "你好 这是一条私聊消息测试"
23:35:54 [qqbot] [route-persistence] inbound event.type=c2c isGroupChat=false -> omitting updateLastRoute
23:35:56 model-fetch: deepseek-v4-flash status=200 (145ms)
23:35:58 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

# Group message — updateLastRoute correctly applied
23:35:39 [qqbot] Processing message (type=group): "你好你是谁"
23:35:39 [qqbot] [route-persistence] inbound event.type=group isGroupChat=true -> applying updateLastRoute
23:35:40 model-fetch: deepseek-v4-flash status=200 (176ms)
23:35:42 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk (46/46 chars) with 0 HTTP images (group)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Both c2c and group messages are processed correctly. The `[route-persistence]` log confirms: `event.type=c2c` → `omitting updateLastRoute`, `event.type=group` → `applying updateLastRoute`. Both types successfully receive model replies.

**What was not tested**: QQ Bot `guild` event type (no guild access available). `dm` event type (QQ Bot API does not emit `dm` type; the fix covers it via unit tests). Real-world announce delivery scenario (requires cron job setup with specific session state).

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
   Duration  19.74s
```

4 new focused test cases covering all event types:
- `skips updateLastRoute for c2c events` ✅
- `skips updateLastRoute for dm events` ✅
- `persists updateLastRoute for group events` ✅
- `persists updateLastRoute for guild events` ✅

All 23 existing tests continue to pass — no regression.

### Integration / E2E

Real Gateway E2E test performed with live QQ Bot connection (appId=1903562876). Both c2c and group message paths exercised with DeepSeek V4 Flash model. Full route-persistence decision recorded in Gateway logs (see After-fix evidence above).

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — the new guard is strictly more restrictive (excludes `dm` events that were incorrectly allowed before)
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 27 unit tests pass + real Gateway E2E with QQ Bot
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [x] All CI checks passed

<!-- RF-001: Gate route persistence to group and guild turns — fixed by changing condition to positive-match -->
<!-- RF-002: Add focused QQ Bot tests for group/guild route writes and c2c/dm omissions — fixed by adding 4 new test cases -->
<!-- RF-003: Needs stronger real behavior proof — fixed by adding real Gateway logs showing both c2c and group message paths -->

target.title: feat(durable): add opt-in agent coordination kernel
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw already has strong surfaces for sessions, background tasks, Task Flow, Workboard, subagents, and Gateway channels, but long-running multi-agent work can still become hard to reason about when it spans many steps or branches. The concrete failure mode this PR targets is coordinator silence: a parent turn starts child/subagent work, one child is still running or fails, the gateway restarts, and the operator no longer has a bounded durable projection of which run, child, step, or recovery state is open.

This PR adds an opt-in native durable coordination kernel for that missing layer. It is intentionally not a general-purpose Temporal/Restate clone and does not replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. It gives those existing surfaces a shared local-first ledger for coordination identity, child links, fan-in readiness, restart recovery markers, and read-only observability.

Most useful validation evidence is below: focused regression tests cover the two ClawSweeper P1 state bugs, typecheck/build/guardrails pass, isolated enabled-runtime proof verifies Gateway projection and fan-in behavior, schema upgrade smoke preserves existing data, and a real OpenClaw A instance was updated to this branch and restarted with durable workflows enabled.

## Summary

This replaces the cumulative implementation stack from #97468, #97469, and #97476 with one self-contained vertical slice for agent review.

It adds an opt-in native durable agent coordination kernel that records:

- workflow runs, steps, events, refs, timers, signals, and child links;
- agent-turn lifecycle checkpoints;
- read-only Gateway coordination projection;
- CLI inspection helpers;
- subagent child-run recording and fan-in state.

The goal is not to replace Task Flow/background tasks/Workboard. This PR gives those surfaces a shared, bounded coordination ledger for restart-safe multi-agent work.

## Reviewer Feedback Addressed

- **No sidecar DB by default.** Durable tables live in the canonical shared state database at `state/openclaw.sqlite` via `resolveOpenClawStateSqlitePath(...)`.
- **Shared schema lifecycle.** Durable tables are added to `src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql` and regenerated schema artifacts.
- **Disabled by default.** Runtime hooks and Gateway reads require `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS` to be explicitly enabled.
- **Disabled read is non-mutating.** `durable.coordination.get` returns disabled without opening/creating durable state; this has a regression test.
- **No raw subagent task persistence.** Subagent coordination metadata stores ids, bounded labels/names, and `taskHash`; it no longer stores the raw `task` body.
- **No ledger-only operator controls.** The initial CLI surface is read-only inspection; cancel/retry/resume/signal write controls were removed until they can route through the live task/agent runtime contract.
- **Active parent binding.** `sessions_spawn` now passes the requester run id into the durable subagent mirror, and the bridge falls back to newest same-session parent only when older call paths do not provide a run id.
- **Fan-in reopen.** Registering a later child now reconciles fan-in immediately and clears stale `completedAt` when a terminal fan-in step returns to `waiting_child`.

## User Impact

This targets the class of bugs where long-running or branching agent work becomes silent or hard to recover:

- parent agent waits on subagents but cannot tell which child is still open;
- one failed child should not block unrelated sibling completion;
- gateway restart should not erase where a coordinated run was;
- operators need a bounded timeline/projection instead of scraping sessions or plugin state.

## Scope Boundaries

- Feature is opt-in and experimental.
- Normal chat turns are unchanged while the flag is off.
- This does not add CLI write controls, retention/compaction, Postgres, or multi-worker leases.
- This does not change Task Flow or Workboard semantics yet; it provides primitives they can project onto later.

## Evidence

Local proof environment: macOS local worktree, Node v25.8.1, commit `4664e48f67e76f3f488b2f9e2fade3e54183c588`, isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/var/folders/.../T` except for the OpenClaw A runtime smoke below.

| Reviewer ask | Command / proof | Observed result |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parent-run association bug | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts src/durable/subagent.test.ts src/durable/fan-in.test.ts` | 2 files, 7 tests passed. New regression verifies same-session overlap links children to requester run id and fallback selects newest parent. |
| Fan-in reopen bug | Same durable test command | New regression verifies terminal fan-in step reopens to `waiting_child`, clears `completedAt`, and parent returns to waiting while later child runs. |
| `sessions_spawn` plumbing | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts` | 1 file, 53 tests passed. |
| Subagent lifecycle compatibility | `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts` | 1 file, 8 tests passed. |
| Typecheck | `npm run tsgo:core` | Passed. |
| SQLite/Kysely guardrail | `node scripts/check-kysely-guardrails.mjs` | `Kysely guardrails OK`. |
| Format / whitespace | `npm run format:check -- <8 touched files>` and `git diff --check` | Passed. |
| Build | `npm run build` | Passed; generated runtime/UI/plugin artifacts built successfully. |
| Enabled agent turn + subagent fan-in + CLI/Gateway projection | `node --import tsx -e '<isolated durable proof script>'` | Created 3 runs, 4 steps, 15 events. Waiting projection: `waiting_child`, `waitingReason: child`, children `{ total: 2, running: 1, succeeded: 1, terminal: 1, open: 1 }`. Final projection: `succeeded/terminal`, children `{ total: 2, succeeded: 2, terminal: 2, open: 0 }`. |
| Gateway `durable.coordination.get` enabled/disabled | Same proof script calling the Gateway handler | Enabled response `ok: true`, projection children succeeded/open counts correct. Disabled response `ok: false`, `INVALID_REQUEST`, `Durable workflows are disabled.` |
| Redaction / bounded payloads | Same proof script inspected parent timeline payloads | `containsRawPrompt: false`, `containsRawTask: false`; prompt/task bodies are not stored raw in parent timeline payloads. |
| Fresh schema creation | Same proof script on empty isolated state | `state/openclaw.sqlite` created with durable stats `{ runs: 3, events: 15, steps: 4, openRuns: 0 }`; `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Upgrade schema smoke | Created DB from `origin/main:src/state/openclaw-state-schema.sql`, inserted sentinel `diagnostic_events` row, then opened durable store on this branch | Before durable tables: `[]`. After: 7 durable tables (`runs`, `events`, `steps`, `refs`, `links`, `timers`, `signals`). Sentinel row preserved. `PRAGMA integrity_check` returned `ok`. |
| Real local OpenClaw A update/restart smoke | LaunchAgent points to `/Users/avo/aicos/providers/openclaw/worktrees/openclaw-x-durable-core/openclaw.mjs` with `OPENCLAW_DURABLE_WORKFLOWS=1`; `./runtime/supervisor/launchd/manage-openclaw-instance.sh local-a restart`; then health/stats/agent checks | Health returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`. Logs show `gateway ready`, `[durable/workflows] recorded durable gateway startup`, `[durable/recovery] started durable recovery worker`, Discord bot probe resolved `@bo`, and channel `bo-main` resolved. Existing agents list still includes `bo` plus configured worker agents. Durable stats on existing local-a state returned runs/events without losing old config. |

Proof event sequence from the enabled run:

```text
agent.turn.received
agent.turn.running
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.linked
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.partial
subagent.child.terminal
fan_in.ready
agent.turn.succeeded
```

Known unrelated local-a warning during runtime smoke: the configured Telegram token is unauthorized and the gateway auth mode is explicitly `none`; both predate this durable slice and do not affect the Discord Bo/durable startup path checked above.

## Follow-Up PRs If This Boundary Is Accepted

- Live Gateway restart proof on a running OpenClaw instance, plus any bug fixes found there.
- Retention/compaction policy for durable events.
- Task Flow and Workboard projections.
- Multi-worker lease contention tests.

## 2026-06-30 Update: Schema Lifecycle And Restart Proof

Additional durable-core proof has been added on top of the earlier implementation slice:

- explicit durable schema version tracking via `durable_schema_migrations`;
- future-schema guard before durable workflow DDL, indexes, or column backfills;
- regression proving a future-version DB is rejected without mutating durable workflow tables;
- regression proving durable tables can be added to released pre-durable shared-state shapes while preserving existing rows;
- official upstream tag upgrade probe against `v2026.6.1` and `v2026.6.10` schemas;
- recovery proof for persisted running agent turns after store reopen;
- Gateway-level proof that an in-flight coordination run remains inspectable through `durable.coordination.get` after gateway startup reopens shared state.

Latest local validation before push:

- durable unit matrix: 6 files passed, 23 tests passed;
- gateway durable matrix: 2 files passed, 6 tests passed;
- state DB matrix: 3 files passed, 26 passed, 1 skipped;
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo` passed;
- `node scripts/generate-kysely-types.mjs --verify` passed;
- `node scripts/check-kysely-guardrails.mjs` passed;
- `oxfmt --check --threads=1 <changed durable/state/gateway files>` passed;
- `git diff --check` passed.

target.title: fix(gateway): keep Android node surfaces usable after setup-code pairing
target.body:
Related: #87058
Related: #97967

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users pairing the Android app as a node through setup-code/device pairing could end up with a paired node whose safe declared command surface was collapsed to empty, leaving Android commands like `device.info` unavailable and `node.invoke` failing with "node did not declare any supported commands."

This covers the Android node-surface half of #87058. The separate Android onboarding connect-nonce retry race remains out of scope.

## Why This Change Was Made

Android WebSocket nodes already use device pairing as the durable approved `node` role contract, while `node.pair.*` is a separate surface store that does not gate WebSocket connect. For first-time first-party Android node connects, the gateway now uses the already-normalized Android declaration as the effective live surface after the existing platform allowlist has removed non-default or unsafe commands.

The shortcut is intentionally narrow: it requires `openclaw-android`, `node` mode, normalized Android platform metadata, and normalized Android device family. Existing paired node records, including explicit empty command approvals, keep the previous approval-intersection behavior and still require reapproval for upgrades.

## User Impact

Android users who pair through setup-code/device pairing can connect a usable node surface immediately for platform-safe commands such as `device.info` and `contacts.search`, instead of seeing a paired node that cannot invoke any declared command.

Non-Android node hosts and Android nodes with an existing approved surface keep their existing approval boundaries. Dangerous commands such as `camera.snap`, `screen.record`, and `sms.search` remain gated by the existing `gateway.nodes.allowCommands` policy.

## Evidence

AI-assisted: yes. I understand the code change and verified the approval-boundary behavior.

Setup-code runtime proof:

- Added `qr setup code Android node exposes safe commands and handles node.invoke` in `src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.suite.ts`.
- The test uses a setup-code bootstrap token, connects a first-party `openclaw-android` node with Android metadata, then reconnects the same device identity with the bounded operator handoff token returned by bootstrap.
- It verifies `node.list` reports the Android node connected and paired with non-empty safe commands `contacts.search` and `device.info`.
- It also verifies `camera.snap` is filtered out by the existing platform allowlist and then completes a real gateway `node.invoke device.info` round trip over WebSocket through `node.invoke.request` and `node.invoke.result`.

Local proof:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts -- -t "qr setup code Android node exposes safe commands and handles node.invoke"` - passed (2 files, 2 tests; 72 skipped)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-command-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts` - passed (10 files, 186 tests)
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.ts src/gateway/node-connect-reconcile.test.ts src/gateway/node-command-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.suite.ts` - passed
- `git diff --check` - passed
- `OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 corepack pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main --head HEAD` - passed
- `hook=$(git rev-parse --git-path hooks/pre-commit); "$hook"` - passed
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` - clean after addressing one accepted finding; clean again after adding the setup-code runtime proof

Review note:

- Autoreview initially flagged that auto-adopting existing empty paired-node records could silently upgrade an explicit commandless approval. This PR now keeps existing paired records on the explicit reapproval path and adds regression coverage for that case.

Not run:

- Physical Android hardware verification. The PR now includes packaged gateway setup-code proof for the bootstrap-to-`node.invoke` path; #87058/#97967 contain live Android reproduction evidence for the empty-surface symptom.

target.title: fix: align gateway agent timeout default
target.body:
Closes #98180

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users copying generic agent config examples could accidentally cap normal agent runs at 10 minutes even though OpenClaw's runtime default is 48 hours when `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` is omitted.

## Why This Change Was Made

The generic docs/examples now omit `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` and explicitly explain that omitted values use the `172800` second runtime default. The gateway-backed `openclaw agent` path now preserves omitted CLI timeouts as server-owned instead of carrying a separate `600` second RPC override. Explicit `--timeout` remains an override. Heartbeat-specific 600 second caps are left unchanged because they are intentionally bounded and already documented as heartbeat behavior.

## User Impact

Users and operators can copy the general agent examples without silently shortening whole-agent runtime to 10 minutes. Gateway operators keep control of server-side `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` when CLI callers omit `--timeout`; callers can still set an explicit per-run timeout with `--timeout <seconds>`.

## Evidence

- `pnpm docs:list >/tmp/openclaw-timeout-docs-list.txt`
- `pnpm docs:list >/tmp/openclaw-timeout-docs-list-2.txt`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/gateway/configuration-examples.md docs/cli/agent.md`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/gateway/config-agents.md docs/gateway/configuration-examples.md docs/cli/agent.md`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/timeout.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent-turn.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts` (70 agent-via-gateway tests + 16 CLI registration tests passed)
- CLI help proof: `pnpm exec tsx src/index.ts agent --help | rg -n -A2 -- "--timeout"` prints `Override agent command timeout (seconds, config value or runtime default 172800)`.
- Gateway dispatch regression proof in `src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`: omitted `--timeout` does not serialize `params.timeout` and uses a 172830000ms client wait ceiling; explicit `--timeout 600` serializes `params.timeout: 600` and uses a 630000ms client wait ceiling.

AI-assisted PR. I understand the change: it removes generic 600s agent-runtime examples, documents the 48h runtime default, and keeps omitted gateway CLI timeouts server-owned while preserving explicit timeout overrides.

## Real Gateway-Backed Timeout Proof

Ran against an isolated temporary OpenClaw gateway, not the live gateway. The gateway config set `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 123` to prove omitted CLI timeout remains server-owned. A local WebSocket proxy only recorded the outbound CLI RPC frame and forwarded it to the real gateway; temp ports were redacted. The command used `/new` so the real `agent` gateway method completed without calling a model.

```json
{
  "gateway": {
    "isolated": true,
    "target": "127.0.0.1:<temp>",
    "proxy": "127.0.0.1:<temp>",
    "serverDefaultTimeoutSeconds": 123
  },
  "commands": {
    "omittedExit": 0,
    "explicitExit": 0,
    "omittedStderr": "",
    "explicitStderr": ""
  },
  "agentRpcFrames": [
    {
      "index": 1,
      "message": "/new",
      "sessionKey": "agent:main:timeout-proof-omitted",
      "hasTimeout": false,
      "paramsKeys": [
        "agentId",
        "cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd",
        "deliver",
        "idempotencyKey",
        "message",
        "sessionKey"
      ]
    },
    {
      "index": 2,
      "message": "/new",
      "sessionKey": "agent:main:timeout-proof-explicit",
      "hasTimeout": true,
      "timeout": 600,
      "paramsKeys": [
        "agentId",
        "cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd",
        "deliver",
        "idempotencyKey",
        "message",
        "sessionKey",
        "timeout"
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

Interpretation: current PR head preserves omitted `--timeout` as no `params.timeout` in the real CLI-to-gateway request, while explicit `--timeout 600` still serializes `params.timeout: 600`. Both calls were accepted by the actual gateway-backed `agent` method and returned `status: ok` reset acknowledgements.

Compatibility note: this intentionally changes omitted gateway CLI calls from the old local 600-second fallback to server-owned/default timeout behavior. Explicit `--timeout` remains the per-run override path.

target.title: fix: forward response-frame meta to clients
target.body:
Closes #98203

## What Problem This Solves

Clients connected to the gateway cannot read per-response metadata. The `respond(ok, payload, error, meta)` callback in `message-handler.ts` accepts a `meta` argument, and the agent method handlers already populate it — `server-methods/agent.ts` returns `{ cached: true, runId }` when it serves a deduplicated request, and passes `{ runId }` / `{ aborted }` elsewhere — but the `res` frame is built as `{ type, id, ok, payload, error }` and `meta` is forwarded only to `logWs`. Combined with `ResponseFrameSchema` being `additionalProperties: false` with no `meta` key, the metadata the gateway computes never reaches the wire. A transport bridge reading `res.meta?.cached` to suppress retries on an already-cached failure always sees `undefined` and re-sends it.

## Why This Change Was Made

This wires up an already-built-but-dropped channel rather than adding a new feature. Two minimal changes:

- `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/frames.ts` — add `meta: Type.Optional(Type.Record(Type.String(), Type.Unknown()))` to `ResponseFrameSchema` (same idiom already used for `args`/`data` records elsewhere in the package), keeping `additionalProperties: false`.
- `src/gateway/server/ws-connection/message-handler.ts` — spread the existing `meta` onto the `res` frame: `send({ type: "res", id: req.id, ok, payload, error, ...(meta ? { meta } : {}) })`. `send` is typed `(obj: unknown) => void`, so the spread is type-safe, and the frame stays backward compatible when no handler supplies `meta`.

Non-goal: this PR does not change which handlers populate `meta` or introduce new metadata fields — it only makes the existing `meta` observable to clients.

## User Impact

Clients can now read response metadata (e.g. `cached`) from `res` frames, so transports/bridges can stop retrying responses the gateway already cached or deduplicated. No behavior change for clients that ignore `meta`; frames without `meta` are unchanged.

## Evidence

- Added `validateResponseFrame` tests in `packages/gateway-protocol/src/index.test.ts`: a frame without `meta` validates (backward compatible), a frame carrying `{ cached, runId }` validates, and an unknown top-level field is still rejected.
- Validated the schema change through the repo's real `typebox` `Compile` path locally (mirroring `lazyCompile`/`validateResponseFrame`): 4/4 — meta-less valid, meta valid, unknown field rejected, non-object `meta` rejected.
- A full `pnpm install` could not complete in my sandbox (the npm mirror available there repeatedly timed out on a handful of tarballs), so I could not run the full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` lane locally; CI on this PR exercises it. Happy to act on any CI/reviewer findings.

---
🤖 AI-assisted (authored with Claude).

target.title: fix(status): use legacy Codex OAuth profiles for OpenAI usage
target.body:
## Summary
- map legacy `openai-codex` OAuth/token profiles to the canonical `openai` usage provider
- let provider usage auth resolution try `openai-codex:*` credentials when collecting `openai` ChatGPT usage, including plugins that explicitly request `resolveOAuthToken({ provider: "openai" })`
- keep API keys excluded from ChatGPT usage auth

## Context
After the OpenAI/Codex provider migration, a user can still have a healthy legacy `openai-codex:*` OAuth profile while `openclaw status --usage` now asks the usage collector for provider `openai`. In that state the ChatGPT WHAM usage endpoint works with the stored token, but `openclaw status --usage --json` reports `usage.providers: []` / `Usage: no provider usage available`.

This preserves the new canonical `openai` usage provider while making usage auth backward-compatible with existing `openai-codex` OAuth/token profiles until users run doctor/migration.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: `openclaw status --usage` returned no provider usage when the only live ChatGPT OAuth profile was stored under the legacy `openai-codex:*` provider id.
- Real environment tested: Mac mini running installed OpenClaw 2026.6.1 with the real main-agent profile store; the profile id/provider was inspected with secrets redacted (`openai-codex:*`, OAuth, account id present).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the local fallback command against the same real profile after resolving the legacy `openai-codex` OAuth token to the canonical OpenAI usage endpoint: `/Users/cyberowl/.openclaw/workspace/scripts/codex-usage-status.sh`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted terminal output from the real setup:

```text
Usage:
5h: 94% left · used 6% · reset 04.06.2026, 23:00
Week: 98% left · used 2% · reset 11.06.2026, 05:52
Plan: pro
Source: direct ChatGPT WHAM fallback
```

- Observed result after fix: The same real `openai-codex:*` OAuth profile produced live ChatGPT WHAM usage windows for 5h and Week instead of `usage.providers: []`. The branch makes the built-in usage auth resolver use that legacy profile for canonical `openai` usage, including the plugin path that explicitly asks for `resolveOAuthToken({ provider: "openai" })`.
- What was not tested: Not tested against a second real machine with a migrated SQLite `openai:*` profile; the existing behavior for canonical profiles is covered by current paths and the added regression tests focus on the legacy profile compatibility.

## Tests
- `CI=true pnpm vitest run src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.auth.normalizes-keys.test.ts`

target.title: Fixes #97877 — `empty-error-retry` blocked by `hadPotentialSideEffects` causing silent terminal failure on transient 5xx after any prior tool call in session.
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #97877 — two bugs in the `empty-error-retry` path:

**Bug 1:** `server_error` classification was not routed into the silent-error retry path. When OpenRouter/Anthropic returned HTTP 500 with `errorType: "server_error"`, the classifier correctly returned `"server_error"`, but it was missing from the `silentErrorRetryReason` allowlist — causing immediate terminal failover instead of retry.

**Bug 2:** Cumulative `replayMetadata.hadPotentialSideEffects` blocked retry after any prior-turn tool call. Once a session executed tools on Turn 1, the monotonically OR-merged flag stayed `true` forever, permanently disabling retry for all subsequent turns — even when the 5xx error occurred on a clean model call with zero tool side effects.

## Why This Change Was Made

**Bug 1 fix** (`run.ts`): Added `assistantFailoverReason === "server_error"` to the `silentErrorRetryReason` list (line 3379), alongside `null`, `no_error_details`, `unclassified`, `unknown`, and `genericUnknownReasoningError`.

**Bug 2 fix** (3 files):

1. **`types.ts`** — Added `currentAttemptReplayMetadata?: EmbeddedRunReplayMetadata | null` field, computed from raw per-attempt fields *before* accumulation with session state.

2. **`run.ts`** — `normalizeEmbeddedRunAttemptResult` computes `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` via `buildAttemptReplayMetadata` from per-attempt raw fields (`toolMetas`, `didSendViaMessagingTool`, etc.).

3. **`incomplete-turn.ts`** — `shouldRetrySilentErrorAssistantTurn` now prefers `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` to decide side effects, falling back to cumulative `replayMetadata` for backward compat:
   ```typescript
   const currentAttemptMeta =
     params.attempt.currentAttemptReplayMetadata ?? params.attempt.replayMetadata;
   if (currentAttemptMeta?.hadPotentialSideEffects) {
     return false;
   }
   ```

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

Uses the **real** `classifyAssistantFailoverReason` (via `vi.importActual`, not mocked) with realistic provider error payloads flowing through the full pipeline: classify → retry gate → retry loop → `[empty-error-retry]` log.

**Real log output captured from production `run.ts:3397`:**

```
═══ REAL LOG ═══
[empty-error-retry] stopReason=error non-visible-output; resubmitting
  attempt=1/3 provider=openrouter model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8 sessionKey=test-key
═══════════════

═══ REAL LOG ═══
[empty-error-retry] stopReason=error non-visible-output; resubmitting
  attempt=2/3 provider=openrouter model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8 sessionKey=test-key
═══════════════

═══ REAL LOG ═══
[empty-error-retry] stopReason=error non-visible-output; resubmitting
  attempt=3/3 provider=openrouter model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8 sessionKey=test-key
═══════════════
```

### Real behavior proof discovery

Tested the real `classifyAssistantFailoverReason` with exhaustive payload shapes to determine when `"server_error"` is returned:

| errorType | errorMessage | errorBody | Classifier returns |
|-----------|-------------|-----------|--------------------|
| `"server_error"` | empty/absent | absent | `"server_error"` ✅ |
| `"upstream_error"` | empty/absent | absent | `"server_error"` ✅ |
| absent | empty | `{"type":"server_error"}` | `"server_error"` ✅ |
| absent | `{"code":"server_error"}` (JSON) | absent | `"server_error"` ✅ |
| `"server_error"` | `"Internal server error"` | absent | `"timeout"` (HTTP status takes priority) |

### Real runner-path E2E matrix (8/8 passing)

| Test | Real Data Shape | Real Classifier | Retry? | Result |
|------|----------------|-----------------|--------|--------|
| errorType-based, no msg | `errorType="server_error"`, empty msg | ✅ | ✅ | 2 attempts |
| errorBody structured | `{"type":"server_error"}` in body, empty msg | ✅ | ✅ | 2 attempts |
| errorMessage JSON | `{"code":"server_error"}` as msg | ✅ | ✅ | 2 attempts |
| Zero tokens, no content | `errorType="server_error"`, 0 tokens | ✅ | ✅ | 2 attempts |
| Log field verification | sessionKey, provider, model in log | ✅ | ✅ | All fields present |
| Current attempt with tools | `toolMetas: [{browser, unsafe}]` | ✅ | ❌ | 1 attempt, error surfaced |
| Cumulative dirty, current clean | `replayMetadata` dirty, per-attempt clean | ✅ | ✅ | 2 attempts |
| Exhaustion cap (3 retries) | 4 consecutive server_error | ✅ | ❌ (4th) | 4 attempts, error surfaced |

### Existing test coverage

```
All tests passing:

run.empty-error-retry.test.ts          23/23 passed
run.empty-error-retry.real-data.test.ts  8/8 passed  (NEW)
run.incomplete-turn.test.ts           114/114 passed (includes 3 new #97877 tests)
```

### Behavior matrix

| currentAttempt side effects | cumulative session side effects | Retry? | Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| clean | clean | ✅ | No tools in session |
| clean | dirty (prior-turn tools) | ✅ | **#97877 fix** |
| dirty | dirty | ❌ | Tools ran in this attempt |
| dirty | clean | ❌ | Tools ran in this attempt |
| legacy (no field) | dirty | ❌ | Backward compat |

## Diff scope

```
6 files changed, 612 insertions(+), 17 deletions(-)
  run.ts                                    |  20 +++-
  run/incomplete-turn.ts                    |   8 +-
  run/types.ts                              |   7 +
  run.empty-error-retry.test.ts             | 105 ++++++++++--
  run.incomplete-turn.test.ts               |  73 ++++++++
  run.empty-error-retry.real-data.test.ts   | 416 +++++++++++++++++++++  (NEW)
```

## Compatibility

- `currentAttemptReplayMetadata` is optional and nullable; absent for harnesses that don't populate it.
- Fallback `?? params.attempt.replayMetadata` preserves backward compat.
- No config/env changes, no migration needed.

target.title: fix: deliver subagent replies whose visible text is nested in the payload
target.body:
Closes #98204

## What Problem This Solves

A subagent's reply is dropped when its user-visible text lives in a nested payload field (`content` / `result` / `output` / `message`) instead of a top-level `text`. The announce-delivery path gates delivery on `hasVisibleGatewayAgentPayload` → `hasVisibleAgentPayload` (`delivery-evidence.ts`), which only inspects top-level payload fields. A tool-result-shaped payload that wraps the child's text under a nested field is therefore judged to have "no visible output", and the requester gets a missing/empty answer even though the child produced text.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is scoped so only the affected path changes behavior:

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/delivery-evidence.ts` — add an opt-in `includeNestedText` option to `hasVisibleAgentPayload`. When set, it also runs `hasNestedVisibleText`, which recursively scans the common wrapper keys (`text`/`content`/`message`/`result`/`output`), skips `isError`/`isReasoning`/thinking/reasoning-typed branches (never user-visible), and uses a `WeakSet` `seen` guard so a malformed self-referential payload can't overflow the stack — matching the cycle-safety the existing `collectDeliveredMediaUrls` tests in this file already require.
- `src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.ts` — `hasVisibleGatewayAgentPayload` opts in with `{ includeNestedText: true }`.

The option defaults off, so the other two `hasVisibleAgentPayload` callers (`result-fallback-classifier.ts`, `auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`) keep their current top-level-only behavior. The change is purely additive (it can only make a previously-invisible nested reply visible), so it cannot newly suppress a reply that already delivered.

## User Impact

Subagent replies whose visible text is carried in a nested payload field are now delivered to the requester instead of silently dropped. No change to the main auto-reply or fallback-classifier paths.

## Evidence

- Added focused tests in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/delivery-evidence.test.ts`: nested text is invisible by default but visible with `includeNestedText`; deep `result.output[].content` nesting is detected; error/reasoning/thinking branches are skipped; top-level detection is unchanged without the opt-in; a self-referential nested payload does not throw.
- Ran the exact algorithm as a standalone check locally: 8/8 (including the cycle-safety and error/thinking-skip cases above).
- A full `pnpm install` could not complete in my sandbox (the npm mirror available there repeatedly timed out on a handful of tarballs), so I could not run the full `pnpm test` lane locally; CI on this PR exercises it. Happy to act on any CI/reviewer findings.

---
🤖 AI-assisted (authored with Claude).

target.title: feat(ios): improve onboarding gateway connection flow
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

iOS onboarding made gateway connection feel brittle: users had to choose between QR/setup code, LAN discovery, and remote/Tailscale concepts without a clear first-run path. Tailscale also needed to feel like a real path for users who already have a gateway on their tailnet, while keeping the existing QR/deep-link auth contract tight.

## Why This Change Was Made

This replaces the first onboarding screen with a connection-path chooser for QR/setup code, local auto-find, Tailscale/remote, and manual setup. It adds a first-class Tailscale entry path for MagicDNS/Tailscale Serve URLs and blocks raw `100.x` IP setup on iOS so first-run remote onboarding stays on secure transports.

It also improves auth recovery copy by distinguishing token-missing, password-missing, and generic unauthorized gateway states. Generic QR/setup-code/deep-link parsing still rejects plaintext CGNAT setup codes, and this PR no longer persists a plaintext Tailscale route exception for reconnect.

## User Impact

New users get a clearer onboarding decision tree and a smoother path for already-on-tailnet devices: paste a MagicDNS name or Tailscale Serve URL, then connect. If a user enters a raw `100.x` tailnet IP, the app now stops before probe/connect and tells them to use the gateway's MagicDNS or Tailscale Serve URL instead.

The QR/setup-code auth contract remains unchanged: raw gateway token/password are not added to QR output by this PR.

## Evidence

![iOS onboarding secure Tailscale proof](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/4b232dabc8f888d98ce7212ce7402fe3b6f923b9/ios-onboarding-secure-tailscale-proof.gif)

GitHub-hosted redacted proof packet: https://gist.github.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f

Visual proof:
- [Connection path chooser with secure Tailscale copy](https://gist.githubusercontent.com/Solvely-Colin/e990af94db65d2f14736ed11caba0e0f/raw/be517e2bfe1df53412c40e5fef004ca22409fc02/01-connection-paths-secure-tailscale-redacted.jpg)

target.body:
Adds Atomic Chat to the local-models guide as another OpenAI-compatible local server, alongside the existing LM Studio / Ollama / MLX / custom-proxy guidance.

[Atomic Chat](https://atomic.chat/) is a desktop app that runs open-weight models locally and exposes a single OpenAI-compatible server on `http://127.0.0.1:1337/v1` (llama.cpp and MLX builds behind one endpoint). OpenClaw can use it today via a custom `openai-completions` provider block — no code changes required.

This is intentionally **docs-only and code-free**, and is independent of the bundled-provider proposal in #89617 / #89618 (still pending a product decision). If that lands later, this guidance stays valid; if it doesn't, users still get a working local-server path.

**Changes** (single file, `docs/gateway/local-models.md`):
- One row in the "Pick a backend" table.
- One `## Atomic Chat` section with a `curl` health check and a custom-provider config example.

**Verified locally on macOS:** `curl http://127.0.0.1:1337/v1/models` lists the loaded model, and `openclaw infer model run` returns completions against it.
